title: Embeddings as Search Keys
concept: An embedding is a compact numeric vector that represents an image's visual content, positioned in a feature space so that visually similar images end up close together — the mathematical foundation of image search.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Why is image retrieval well-suited to newly added products with zero training data?

options.text: It compares visual similarity directly without needing category-specific retraining
It automatically generates fake training photos
It only works if every product already has a label

exercises.explain: Yes! Similarity comparison sidesteps the need for category-specific training.

cards.title: Turning a picture into a list of numbers
cards.body: How does a computer compare 'how similar' two images look? It can't compare raw pixels directly (a shifted or slightly cropped photo would look 'completely different' pixel-by-pixel). We need a smarter numeric summary.

cards.title: Embeddings
cards.body: An embedding is a compact numeric vector (e.g. 512 numbers) produced by a CNN's late layers that summarizes an image's visual content. Crucially, the embedding space is trained so that visually similar images end up close together, and dissimilar images end up far apart.

cards.title: GPS coordinates for meaning
cards.body: Think of embeddings like GPS coordinates, but for visual meaning instead of physical location. Two houses on the same street have GPS coordinates close together. Two photos of similar-looking sneakers have embedding vectors close together in this 'meaning space' — even if the actual pixels differ a lot.

cards.title: Matching sneaker photos
cards.body: A red sneaker photo and a slightly different red sneaker photo (different angle, different lighting) produce embedding vectors that land close together in the embedding space, even though their raw pixel values are quite different.

cards.title: Embeddings aren't compressed images
cards.body: It's a myth that an embedding is just a 'shrunk down' version of the image you could decompress back to pixels. An embedding discards most raw pixel information on purpose, keeping only the high-level visual meaning needed for comparison — you generally can't reconstruct the original image from it.

cards.title: Raw Pixels vs. Embedding Vectors
cards.body: Why embeddings work better for similarity search:

left.text:
Sensitive to shift/crop/lighting
Two similar photos can look 'far apart'
Poor similarity signal

right.text:
Captures high-level visual meaning
Similar photos land close together
Strong similarity signal

exercises.prompt: What is an embedding in the context of image retrieval?

options.text: A compact numeric vector summarizing an image's visual content, positioned so similar images are close together
A compressed but fully reconstructable copy of the original image
The exact file size of an image in bytes
A list of bounding box coordinates

exercises.explain: Correct! Embeddings are compact vectors capturing high-level visual meaning.

exercises.prompt: True or false: You can generally reconstruct the exact original image from its embedding vector.
exercises.explain: False. Embeddings discard most raw pixel detail, keeping only high-level visual meaning needed for comparison.

exercises.sentence: In embedding space, visually similar images end up ___ together.
exercises.explain: The embedding space is trained so similar images land near each other.

exercises.prompt: Why does comparing raw pixel values directly give a poor similarity signal for two photos of the same sneaker taken from different angles?

options.text: A shift in angle or lighting changes pixel values a lot, even though the visual content is still very similar
Raw pixels are always identical for the same object regardless of angle
Sneakers cannot be photographed from multiple angles

exercises.explain: Correct! Raw pixels are highly sensitive to viewpoint/lighting, unlike learned embeddings.

exercises.prompt: You want to build a 'find similar products' feature. Should you compare products using raw pixel differences or learned embedding vectors?

options.text: Embedding vectors, since they capture high-level visual similarity robust to angle, lighting, and minor cropping differences
Raw pixel differences, since they are always more accurate than any learned representation
Neither; product similarity cannot be computed from images at all
File size comparison, since larger files are always more similar

exercises.explain: Exactly! Embeddings robustly capture visual meaning where raw pixels fail. Next: how to actually search through millions of these embedding vectors quickly.
